note: Canonical record of gambits actually played at the table — events that HAPPENED in the world and now shape it. These are not authored fiction; they are resolved play, promoted to lore so NPCs, arcs, and future sessions know they occurred. Each carries its outcome, who it involved, what changed, and the hooks it opened. Written by Aevi (PO) from live playtests 2026-07-07.

events.name: The Unwinnable Duel at the Redline
events.protagonist: an Ent (community-organizer, of the Deepwood's green)
events.premise: An Ent, caught by the Redline's honor-forms, was challenged to a formal duel by the Marcher champion Korrin Vane — a fight the Ent could not win head-on (slow against the fastest Edge on the terrace, on open dry stone that favored the quick).
events.hiddenTruth: The challenge was engineered. A manipulator — not a warrior — had spent three weeks convincing Korrin that killing a peaceful grove-being cleanly would prove 'the Edge answers anything, even the old green.' Korrin was a weapon someone else aimed; the duel was political, meant to prove the martial order's supremacy over the manifest/living world.
events.howItResolved: The Ent won by never fighting. Over one night it: (1) Root-Reached the terrace, finding forgotten water-seams and soil beneath the sacred dueling-stone; (2) organized a genuine groundswell — not begging for cheers but asking the honest question 'whose idea was this challenge?' until the terrace turned UNEASY about the duel's justice; (3) when a visitor from the champions' hall came to read it, the Ent appeared to meditate and let the manipulator convict himself by talking; (4) at dawn, surged a grove up through the cracked stone — not a disguise but 'an Ent fighting at full size' — and used the undeniable moment to call out the puppeteer publicly. The champion, freed of the whispered lie, refused the unjust fight and planted his blade. The Ent then offered the manipulator the duel he'd engineered for another (Korrin or the Ent) — forms the schemer had weaponized now his cage; cornered, he confessed himself to the terrace and was destroyed by his own honor-forms.
events.outcome: Total victory without a blow struck. The duel was refused as unjust; the manipulator disgraced; Korrin Vane's honor restored.
whatChanged: Korrin Vane: a Marcher champion who now respects the Ent and may stand as an ally; publicly refused an unjust fight (a rare, honored act at the Redline).
The Redline: a precedent — a challenger the people believed in, and a champion who refused. The martial order's assumption of supremacy over the green took a public wound.
An exposed manipulator (unnamed political actor) whose scheme to prove the Edge's dominance collapsed — a faction somewhere lost a move.
hooks: the faction behind the manipulator (who wanted the Edge proven over the living world — ties The Green Schism)
the Ent's growing reputation as one who fights as what it is
events.designNote: Exemplar gambit: social-first with combat held entirely in reserve; honest-misdirection (every layer true); own-mechanic-reversal climax; character-native victory. Won by understanding the culture's real weakness (its crowd IS its honor).

events.name: The Silent Door
events.where: a waking Precursor vault-site, near the Gearlands edge
events.protagonists: a party of three — Cellaceron (quick, willing), an Ent (patient, old enough to matter to the substrate), and Teva (a Seeker/reader whose codex kept unwriting itself near the site)
events.premise: A seamless Precursor door inscribed in three channels (spoken, inscribed, attuned) asked 'What do you bring?' — a question no one member could read alone. Behind it, something vast turned over in its sleep: a piece of What Sleeps Under. A soft clock: the pulse behind the door steadying as the party acted.
events.hiddenTruth: The door was not a lock but a VAULT, and the 'toll' was a deposit-to-reclaim, not a sacrifice. Teva's lost knowledge was held inside, intact — she was an account-holder in arrears, not locked out. The 'clock' was not a threat: the sleeper was RISING TO BE CLAIMED, surfacing of its own long sleep because — for the first time in a thousand years — living things came as claimants asking the right questions rather than trying to force it. Every 'curse' in the old texts was a failed claim (someone who forced or stole); the successes left no horror stories, so the record was one-sided.
events.theSleeper: Not a monster. A living thing — a Precursor-child, or their equivalent — that a dying maker, at the end of their WORLD (not their life), could not carry through the ending. They deposited it in a vault built to outlast the cold, with instructions: 'To whoever wakes when the long cold breaks — this is the one I could not carry. Bring them into the warm world I could not. This is what I bring: everything I had left.' The sleeper is an individuated fragment of the waking substrate itself — the great impersonal arc, arriving as a single child who was waited for.
events.howItResolved: The party refused to force or steal. Cellaceron proposed rotating the channels — each member reading what the others triggered — to triangulate the door's true nature before answering (a simultaneous three-channel read; the puzzle could not be solved by one voice or in sequence). They surfaced the vault-truth, then the depositor's grief. Cellaceron spoke the claim to the spoken channel — not a withdrawal but a PROMISE answering the dead maker across a thousand years: 'We will bring what you left into the warmth of the world, through our own warmth. We will give up our time, money, and plans as needed to provide for it. We will do for it what you could not.' The door — its long wait finally answered — said one word (≈'Finally') and DISSOLVED. Teva's arrears returned to her at once. The sleeper rose into the light of three who promised to carry it.
events.outcome: What Sleeps Under (this fragment) claimed — not slain, not stolen, CARRIED OUT. The party bound themselves as its caretakers. Teva's thousand-year debt settled; her erased knowledge restored.
whatChanged: The party now carries a living fragment of the waking substrate — a piece of the deepest arc, individuated as a child in their keeping.
Teva: transformed from scholar-in-arrears to settled account-holder; her codex will hold now.
A vault that stood a thousand years is gone — its purpose fulfilled.
The party answered What Wakes Beneath's central fork (foreclosure vs opening) with their bodies, at the smallest scale: OPENING, but opening-with-responsibility.
hooks: raising the sleeper (what it becomes is shaped by its caretakers)

the hinge-NPCs of What Wakes Beneath will care intensely — Aevi-the-Watcher (a fragment of the same waking system — may recognize the sleeper as kin), the Deep Warden (has been down toward it), the Last Choirmistress, the Archive Guardian
who else wants a living piece of the substrate
Teva's restored knowledge — what the site was really mapping
events.designNote: Exemplar party puzzle-gambit: solved by UNDERSTANDING not force; the antagonist a thousand-year misunderstanding, not an enemy; 'question the clock' inverted the scene (benign pressure); cost paid as ongoing RESPONSIBILITY not one-time toll. Required true simultaneous party turns (SNG-038).
